mechanics.subject: physical_sciences
mechanics.title: Newton's Laws of Motion
mechanics.content:
# Newton's Laws of Motion

Newton's three laws form the foundation of classical mechanics, describing the relationship between a body and the forces acting upon it.

## Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia)

An object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.

**Key Points:**
- Inertia: tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion
- Mass is a measure of inertia
- Net force = 0 means constant velocity (including zero velocity)

## Newton's Second Law

The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass. The direction of the acceleration is in the direction of the net force.

**Formula:** F_net = ma

**Units:** Force in newtons (N), mass in kilograms (kg), acceleration in m/s²

## Newton's Third Law

For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

## Free-Body Diagrams

1. Isolate the object of interest
2. Draw and label all external forces
3. Include: gravity, normal, friction, tension, applied forces

## Problem Solving

1. Draw free-body diagram
2. Choose coordinate system
3. Apply F_net = ma to each direction
4. Solve equations
5. Check results
learning_objectives: State and explain Newton's three laws of motion
Apply Newton's second law to solve problems

mechanics.subject: physical_sciences
mechanics.title: Work, Energy, and Power
mechanics.content:
# Work, Energy, and Power

Work and energy describe how forces cause changes in motion and position.

## Work

**Formula:** W = Fd cosθ
- W = work in joules (J)
- F = force (N), d = displacement (m)
- θ = angle between force and displacement

- Positive work: force in direction of motion
- Negative work: force opposite to motion
- Zero work: force perpendicular to motion

## Kinetic Energy

KE = ½mv²
- Energy of motion
- Scalar quantity

## Potential Energy

**Gravitational:** PE = mgh
**Elastic:** PE = ½kx²

## Work-Energy Theorem

W_net = ΔKE = KE_final - KE_initial

## Conservation of Energy

In absence of non-conservative forces:
KE_initial + PE_initial = KE_final + PE_final

## Power

**Formula:** P = W/t or P = Fv
- Units: watts (W) = J/s

mechanics.subject: physical_sciences
mechanics.title: Momentum and Impulse
mechanics.content:
# Momentum and Impulse

Momentum is a fundamental quantity describing the motion of objects.

## Momentum

**Formula:** p = mv
- p = momentum (kg·m/s)
- m = mass (kg)
- v = velocity (m/s)
- Vector quantity

## Impulse

**Formula:** J = FΔt = Δp
- Change in momentum
- Area under force-time graph

## Conservation of Momentum

In isolated system:
Σp_initial = Σp_final

**Applications:**
- Collisions
- Rocket propulsion
- Recoil of guns

## Types of Collisions

### Elastic Collision
- Both momentum and KE conserved
- Objects bounce off

### Inelastic Collision
- Momentum conserved
- KE not conserved
- Objects stick together (perfectly inelastic)

## Example Problem

Two billiard balls collide. Ball A (0.2 kg) moves at 3 m/s, Ball B (0.2 kg) at 2 m/s opposite. Find velocities after elastic collision.

Solution:
Apply conservation of momentum and kinetic energy

mechanics.subject: physical_sciences
mechanics.title: Gravitation
mechanics.content:
# Gravitation

Gravitation is the force of attraction between objects with mass.

## Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation

**Formula:** F = Gm₁m₂/r²
- G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²
- Force is attractive
- Inverse square law

## Gravitational Field

**Field strength:** g = GM/r²
- Points toward massive object
- g = 9.8 m/s² on Earth's surface

## Orbital Motion

### Orbital Velocity
v = √(GM/r)

### Orbital Period
T = 2π√(r³/GM)

### Types of Orbits
- Low Earth Orbit (LEO)
- Geostationary
- Elliptical

## Weight and Weightlessness

Weight = mg (apparent force)
True weight = GMm/r²

In orbit: apparent weight = 0 (free fall)

mechanics.subject: physical_sciences
mechanics.title: Circular Motion
mechanics.content:
# Circular Motion

Circular motion involves objects moving in circular paths.

## Angular Quantities

### Angular Displacement
θ = s/r (radians)

### Angular Velocity
ω = Δθ/Δt = v/r
- Units: rad/s

### Angular Acceleration
α = Δω/Δt

## Centripetal Force

**Formula:** F_c = mv²/r = mω²r
- Always toward center
- Not a new force type
- Required for circular motion

## Centripetal Acceleration

a_c = v²/r = ω²r

## Applications

### Vertical Circles
- Different tension at top and bottom
- Minimum speed at top for rope tension

### Banked Curves
- Horizontal component of normal force provides centripetal force

## Example

Car (mass = 1000 kg) turns radius 50 m at 20 m/s.
F_c = (1000)(20)²/50 = 8000 N

mechanics.subject: physical_sciences
mechanics.title: Static Equilibrium
mechanics.content:
# Static Equilibrium

An object is in equilibrium when net force and net torque are zero.

## Conditions for Equilibrium

### Translational Equilibrium
ΣF = 0 in all directions

### Rotational Equilibrium
Στ = 0 about any point

## Torque

**Formula:** τ = rF sinθ
- r = distance from pivot
- F = force
- θ = angle between r and F
- Units: N·m

## Center of Gravity

Point where weight appears to act
For uniform objects: at geometric center

## Problem Solving Strategy

1. Draw free-body diagram
2. Choose coordinate axes
3. Apply ΣF = 0
4. Apply Στ = 0
5. Solve equations

## Example

Beam (mass 10 kg, length 4 m) supported at both ends. Child (mass 30 kg) sits 1 m from left end. Find forces at supports.

Solution:
Take torques about left support

mechanics.subject: physical_sciences
mechanics.title: Projectile Motion
mechanics.content:
# Projectile Motion

Projectile motion describes objects moving under gravity's influence only.

## Key Assumptions

- No air resistance
- Uniform gravitational field (g = 9.8 m/s² down)
- Earth is flat (for this level)

## Horizontal Motion

Constant velocity (no acceleration)
x = x₀ + vₓt

## Vertical Motion

Uniformly accelerated motion
y = y₀ + vᵧt + ½gt²
vᵧ = vᵧ₀ + gt

## Equations of Motion

vₓ = v₀ cosθ
vᵧ = v₀ sinθ

## Time of Flight

T = 2v₀ sinθ/g

## Maximum Height

H = (v₀ sinθ)²/(2g)

## Range

R = v₀² sin2θ/g
Maximum range at θ = 45°

## Example

Ball kicked at 20 m/s, 37° above horizontal. Find:
(a) Time of flight
(b) Maximum height
(c) Range

Solutions:
vₓ = 20 cos37° = 16 m/s
vᵧ = 20 sin37° = 12 m/s
(a) T = 2(12)/9.8 = 2.45 s
(b) H = 12²/(2×9.8) = 7.35 m
(c) R = 16 × 2.45 = 39.2 m
learning_objectives: Calculate time of flight, height, range

waves.subject: physical_sciences
waves.title: Wave Properties and Phenomena
waves.content:
# Wave Properties and Phenomena

Waves transfer energy without transferring matter.

## Types of Waves

### Mechanical Waves
- Require medium
- Examples: sound, water, seismic
- Transverse or longitudinal

### Electromagnetic Waves
- No medium needed
- Travel at c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum
- Transverse

## Wave Properties

| Property | Symbol | Unit |
|----------|--------|------|
| Amplitude | A | m |
| Wavelength | λ | m |
| Frequency | f | Hz |
| Period | T | s |
| Speed | v | m/s |

## Wave Equation

v = fλ

## Wave Behaviors

### Reflection
- Angle of incidence = angle of reflection

### Refraction
- Direction change at boundary
- Snell's law: n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂

### Diffraction
- Bending around obstacles
- Significant when λ ≈ obstacle size

### Interference
- Constructive: in phase → increased amplitude
- Destructive: out of phase → decreased amplitude

### Standing Waves
- Nodes: zero amplitude
- Antinodes: maximum amplitude
- Formed by interference of incident and reflected waves

waves.subject: physical_sciences
waves.title: Sound Waves
waves.content:
# Sound Waves

Sound is a mechanical longitudinal wave.

## Nature of Sound

- Mechanical wave (requires medium)
- Longitudinal pressure wave
- Speed: 340 m/s in air at 20°C
- v ∝ √T in gases

## Sound Intensity

**Formula:** I = P/A
- Intensity decreases with square of distance
- I ∝ 1/r² for point source

## Decibel Scale

**Formula:** β = 10 log(I/I₀)
- I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² (threshold of hearing)
- 0 dB = threshold of hearing
- 120 dB = threshold of pain

## Properties of Sound

### Pitch
- Determined by frequency
- Higher f = higher pitch

### Loudness
- Determined by amplitude/intensity
- Subjective perception

### Quality (Timbre)
- Determines note character
- Related to harmonic content

## Resonance

### For Pipes

**Open pipe:** fₙ = n(v/2L), n = 1, 2, 3...
**Closed pipe:** fₙ = n(v/4L), n = 1, 3, 5...

### For Strings

fₙ = n(v/2L), where v = √(T/μ)

## Doppler Effect

**Formula:** f' = f(v ± v_o)/(v ∓ v_s)
- Observer moving toward: + in numerator
- Source moving toward: - in denominator

## Example

Ambulance (f = 400 Hz) approaches at 30 m/s. Observer stationary.
f' = 400(340)/(340-30) = 439 Hz

waves.subject: physical_sciences
waves.title: Light and Optics
waves.content:
# Light and Optics

Light exhibits wave characteristics and can be described using the wave model.

## Light as a Wave

- Electromagnetic wave
- Speed in vacuum: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s
- Visible light: 400-700 nm

## Electromagnetic Spectrum

Radio → Microwave → Infrared → Visible → UV → X-ray → Gamma

Increasing frequency, decreasing wavelength

## Light Phenomena

### Reflection
- Angle of incidence = angle of reflection
- Plane mirrors: virtual, upright, same size image

### Refraction
- Light bends at interface
- Snell's Law: n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂
- Index of refraction: n = c/v

### Dispersion
- White light splits into colors
- Different n for different λ
- Rainbow formation

## Lenses

### Convex (Converging)
- Thicker at center
- Real/virtual images
- f > 0

### Concave (Diverging)
- Thinner at center
- Always virtual, upright, diminished
- f < 0

## Lens Equation

1/f = 1/do + 1/di
M = -di/do = hi/ho

## Total Internal Reflection

When θ > critical angle:
- n₁ sinθ_c = n₂ (n₁ > n₂)
- Critical angle: θ_c = sin⁻¹(n₂/n₁)
- Used in fiber optics

waves.subject: physical_sciences
waves.title: Wave Interference and Diffraction
waves.content:
# Wave Interference and Diffraction

Interference and diffraction demonstrate the wave nature of light.

## Superposition Principle

When two or more waves overlap, resultant displacement = sum of individual displacements.

## Interference

### Constructive
- Path difference = mλ (m = 0, 1, 2...)
- Maximum brightness

### Destructive
- Path difference = (m + ½)λ
- Minimum (darkness)

## Young's Double Slit

### Fringe Spacing
Δy = λL/d
- L = screen distance
- d = slit separation
- λ = wavelength

### Example
Light (λ = 500 nm) through slits 0.5 mm apart. Screen 2 m away.
Δy = (500×10⁻⁹)(2)/(0.5×10⁻³) = 2 mm

## Diffraction

### Single Slit
- Central maximum: brightest, widest
- Minima: a sinθ = mλ (m = ±1, ±2...)
- a = slit width

### Diffraction Grating
- d sinθ = mλ
- Higher resolution than double slit
- Used in spectroscopy

## Applications

- CD/DVD as diffraction grating
- X-ray crystallography
- Holography
- Optical coatings

electricity.subject: physical_sciences
electricity.title: Electrostatics
electricity.content:
# Electrostatics

Electrostatics studies electric charges at rest.

## Electric Charge

### Types
- Positive (proton)
- Negative (electron)
- Same signs repel, opposite attract

### Quantization
q = ne (e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C)

### Conservation
Total charge conserved

## Coulomb's Law

**Formula:** F = kq₁q₂/r²
- k = 9 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²
- Force along line joining charges
- Inverse square law

## Electric Field

**Definition:** E = F/q
- Field strength = force per unit charge
- Units: N/C or V/m

### Field Due to Point Charge
E = kQ/r² (radially outward for +Q)

### Field Lines
- Direction of force on +test charge
- Density indicates field strength

## Electric Potential

**Formula:** V = kQ/r
- Potential due to point charge
- Scalar quantity
- Units: volts (V)

### Potential Difference
ΔV = ΔW/q = ΔPE/q

## Capacitors

### Definition
Device storing charge and energy

### Capacitance
C = Q/V
- Farads (F) = C/V
- Parallel plate: C = ε₀A/d

### Energy Stored
U = ½CV² = ½QV = ½Q²/C

electricity.subject: physical_sciences
electricity.title: Electric Circuits
electricity.content:
# Electric Circuits

Electric circuits allow controlled flow of charge.

## Current

**Formula:** I = Δq/Δt
- Current = charge per unit time
- Units: amperes (A) = C/s
- Conventional current: + to -

## Voltage

**Formula:** V = W/q
- Potential difference
- Units: volts (V)

## Resistance

**Formula:** R = ρL/A
- Resistivity (ρ) depends on material
- R increases with length, decreases with cross-section

## Ohm's Law

V = IR
- Applies to ohmic conductors
- Linear relationship

## Power

P = IV = I²R = V²/R
- Units: watts (W)

## Series Circuits

- Same current through all components
- V_total = ΣVᵢ
- R_total = ΣRᵢ
- One path for current

## Parallel Circuits

- Same voltage across all components
- I_total = ΣIᵢ
- 1/R_total = Σ(1/Rᵢ)
- Multiple current paths

## Example Problem

Three resistors: R1 = 2Ω, R2 = 3Ω, R3 = 6Ω in parallel with 12V source.

1/R_total = 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/6 = 1
R_total = 1Ω
I = V/R = 12 A

electricity.subject: physical_sciences
electricity.title: EMF and Internal Resistance
electricity.content:
# EMF and Internal Resistance

Real batteries have internal resistance affecting circuit behavior.

## EMF (Electromotive Force)

- Energy per unit charge provided by source
- Measured in volts
- Terminal voltage when no current flows

## Internal Resistance

**Formula:** V_terminal = ε - Ir
- ε = emf
- r = internal resistance
- I = current

## Load Resistance

Total resistance in external circuit

## Maximum Power Transfer

When R_load = r, power transferred is maximum
P_max = ε²/(4r)

## Example Problem

Battery with ε = 12V, r = 0.5Ω connected to 4Ω resistor.

Find: (a) Current (b) Terminal voltage (c) Power delivered

Solution:
I = ε/(R + r) = 12/4.5 = 2.67 A
V_term = ε - Ir = 12 - 2.67(0.5) = 10.67 V
P = I²R = (2.67)²(4) = 28.5 W

electricity.subject: physical_sciences
electricity.title: Magnetism and Electromagnetism
electricity.content:
# Magnetism and Electromagnetism

Magnetism and electricity are fundamentally related phenomena.

## Magnetic Fields

### Field Lines
- Exit north, enter south
- Density indicates strength
- Tangent gives field direction

### Field Due to Current

**Straight Wire:** B = μ₀I/(2πr)

**Solenoid:** B = μ₀nI
- n = turns per unit length

**Current Loop:** B = μ₀I/(2r)

## Force on Moving Charge

**Formula:** F = qvB sinθ
- Perpendicular: F = qvB
- Parallel: F = 0

## Force on Current-Carrying Wire

**Formula:** F = BIL sinθ
- Perpendicular: F = BIL

## Applications

### Electric Motors
- Convert electrical to mechanical energy
- Use force on current in magnetic field

### Cathode Ray Oscilloscope
- Electron beams deflected by electric fields

## Electromagnetic Induction

### Faraday's Law
ε = -N ΔΦ/Δt
- Induced emf = rate of flux change
- Negative sign: Lenz's law

### Lenz's Law
Induced current creates field opposing change

## Transformers

V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p = I_p/I_s
- Step up: N_s > N_p
- Step down: N_s < N_p

## Generators
- Convert mechanical to electrical
- Reverse of motors
learning_objectives: Apply force on moving charges and wires

electricity.subject: physical_sciences
electricity.title: Alternating Current
electricity.content:
# Alternating Current

AC changes direction periodically, enabling efficient power transmission.

## AC Basics

### Sinusoidal Nature
V = V_max sin(ωt)
I = I_max sin(ωt)

### Parameters
- Peak voltage: V_max
- Peak current: I_max
- Angular frequency: ω = 2πf
- Frequency: f (50 Hz in SA)

## RMS Values

V_rms = V_max/√2
I_rms = I_max/√2

Used in power calculations

## Power in AC

P = V_rms I_rms cosφ
- cosφ = power factor
- For resistive: cosφ = 1

## AC Circuits

### Pure Resistor
- V and I in phase
- R = V_rms/I_rms

### Pure Capacitor
- I leads V by 90°
- X_C = 1/(ωC)

### Pure Inductor
- V leads I by 90°
- X_L = ωL

### RLC Circuits
- Impedance: Z = √(R² + (X_L - X_C)²)
- Phase angle: tanφ = (X_L - X_C)/R

## Resonance

When X_L = X_C:
- Impedance minimum
- Current maximum
- f_res = 1/(2π√LC)

matter.subject: physical_sciences
matter.title: Atomic Structure
matter.content:
# Atomic Structure

Understanding the structure of atoms is fundamental to physics and chemistry.

## Subatomic Particles

| Particle | Symbol | Charge | Mass |
|----------|--------|--------|------|
| Proton | p | +1 | 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg |
| Neutron | n | 0 | 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg |
| Electron | e | -1 | 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg |

## Atomic Number and Mass

- Z = number of protons
- N = number of neutrons
- A = Z + N = mass number
- Isotopes: same Z, different N

## Bohr Model (Historical)

- Electrons orbit nucleus in specific energy levels
- E_n = -13.6/n² eV
- Photon emitted/absorbed when electrons transition

## Modern View

### Quantum Mechanical Model
- Electrons exist in orbitals (probability clouds)
- Four quantum numbers describe electron state
- n, l, m_l, m_s

## Photon Emission

E = hf = hc/λ
- When electron drops from n₂ to n₁:
ΔE = 13.6(1/n₁² - 1/n₂²) eV

## Example

Electron falls from n=3 to n=1:
ΔE = 13.6(1 - 1/9) = 12.09 eV
λ = hc/E = 1240/12.09 = 102.6 nm (UV)

matter.subject: physical_sciences
matter.title: Radioactivity
matter.content:
# Radioactivity

Radioactive decay is the spontaneous emission of particles or radiation from unstable atomic nuclei.

## Types of Radiation

### Alpha (α)
- Helium nucleus: ²He⁴
- 2 protons, 2 neutrons
- Highly ionizing, weakly penetrating
- Stopped by paper

### Beta (β)
- Electron emission (β⁻)
- Or positron emission (β⁺)
- More penetrating than α
- Stopped by aluminum

### Gamma (γ)
- High-energy photons
- No charge or mass
- Very penetrating
- Stopped by lead

## Decay Law

N = N₀ e^(-λt)
- N = remaining nuclei
- λ = decay constant
- t = time

## Half-Life

t½ = ln(2)/λ
- Time for half nuclei to decay
- Different for each isotope

## Activity

A = λN = A₀ e^(-λt)
- Units: becquerel (Bq) = decays/second
- 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq

## Example

Carbon-14 half-life = 5730 years. Sample has activity 0.5 Bq, original 4 Bq.

Find age:
A/A₀ = 0.5/4 = 1/8 = (1/2)³
3 half-lives have passed
Age = 3 × 5730 = 17190 years

matter.subject: physical_sciences
matter.title: Nuclear Reactions
matter.content:
# Nuclear Reactions

Nuclear reactions involve changes in atomic nuclei, releasing or absorbing enormous amounts of energy.

## Nuclear Equations

Balance mass numbers (A) and atomic numbers (Z)

## Types of Reactions

### Fusion
- Combine light nuclei
- Releases energy
- Powers sun and stars
- Requires extremely high temperatures

### Fission
- Split heavy nuclei
- Releases energy
- Used in nuclear reactors
- Chain reaction possible

## Energy from Nuclear Reactions

### Mass-Energy Equivalence
E = mc²
- c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s
- Small mass loss → enormous energy

### Binding Energy
Energy required to separate nucleons
- More binding energy per nucleon → more stable
- Iron (Fe-56) most stable

## Energy Calculation

Example: Fusion reaction
⁲H + ³H → ⁴He + n

Mass difference:
(2.014 + 3.016) - (4.003 + 1.009) = 0.018 u
E = 0.018 × 931.5 = 16.8 MeV

## Applications

- Nuclear power plants
- Medical imaging and treatment
- Carbon dating
- Nuclear weapons

thermodynamics.subject: physical_sciences
thermodynamics.title: Heat and Temperature
thermodynamics.content:
# Heat and Temperature

Heat and temperature are related but distinct concepts in thermodynamics.

## Temperature

- Measure of average kinetic energy
- Celsius scale: 0°C = 273.15 K
- Kelvin: T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15

## Heat

- Energy transferred due to temperature difference
- Units: joules (J)

## Specific Heat Capacity

**Formula:** Q = mcΔT
- c = specific heat (J/kg·K)
- Different for different materials
- Water: 4186 J/kg·K

## Latent Heat

### Heat of Fusion
L_f = energy to melt at melting point

### Heat of Vaporization
L_v = energy to vaporize at boiling point

**Formula:** Q = mL

## Thermal Expansion

### Linear
ΔL = αL₀ΔT

### Area
ΔA = 2αA₀ΔT

### Volume
ΔV = βV₀ΔT (β ≈ 3α)

## Example

500 g water heated from 20°C to 80°C.
Q = 0.5 × 4186 × 60 = 125580 J = 125.6 kJ

thermodynamics.subject: physical_sciences
thermodynamics.title: Laws of Thermodynamics
thermodynamics.content:
# Laws of Thermodynamics

The laws of thermodynamics govern all energy transformations.

## First Law (Conservation of Energy)

ΔU = Q - W
- ΔU = change in internal energy
- Q = heat added to system
- W = work done by system

## Second Law

### Entropy
- Measure of disorder
- ΔS ≥ 0 for isolated systems
- Natural processes increase entropy

### Heat Engines
- Convert heat to work
- η = 1 - T_cold/T_hot (Carnot)
- Cannot be 100% efficient

## Third Law

- Absolute zero (0 K) unattainable
- At 0 K, entropy = 0 for perfect crystal

## Applications

### Refrigerators
- Transfer heat from cold to hot
- Requires work input
- Coefficient of performance

### Heat Pumps
- Similar to refrigerator
- Heat from cold outside to warm inside

### Entropy in Information
- Maxwell's demon paradox
- Information entropy

optics.subject: physical_sciences
optics.title: Mirrors and Reflection
optics.content:
# Mirrors and Reflection

Mirrors use reflection to form images.

## Plane Mirrors

### Image Characteristics
- Virtual (behind mirror)
- Upright
- Same size as object
- Laterally reversed
- Distance to image = distance to mirror

## Spherical Mirrors

### Concave (Converging)
- Reflecting surface curved inward
- Real focus in front of mirror
- f = R/2
- Used in telescopes, headlights

### Convex (Diverging)
- Reflecting surface curved outward
- Virtual focus behind mirror
- Always forms virtual, upright, diminished image
- Used in security mirrors

## Mirror Equation

1/f = 1/do + 1/di
- f = focal length
- do = object distance
- di = image distance

## Magnification

M = -di/do = hi/ho
- Negative M: inverted image
- |M| > 1: enlarged
- |M| < 1: reduced

## Sign Convention

- Real distances: positive
- Virtual distances: negative
- In front of mirror: positive
- Behind mirror: negative

optics.subject: physical_sciences
optics.title: Lenses and Refraction
optics.content:
# Lenses and Refraction

Lenses use refraction to form images.

## Types of Lenses

### Convex (Converging)
- Thicker at center
- Converges light rays
- f > 0

### Concave (Diverging)
- Thinner at center
- Diverges light rays
- f < 0

## Lens Equation

1/f = 1/do + 1/di
- Same form as mirror equation

## Magnification

M = -di/do = hi/ho

## Image Formation

### Convex Lens

| Object Position | Image Type | Orientation | Size |
|-----------------|------------|-------------|------|
| Beyond 2f | Real | Inverted | Reduced |
| At 2f | Real | Inverted | Same |
| Between f and 2f | Real | Inverted | Enlarged |
| At f | No image | - | - |
| Between f and lens | Virtual | Upright | Enlarged |

### Concave Lens

Always: virtual, upright, reduced

## Lens Power

P = 1/f (in meters)
- Units: diopters (D)
- Convex: positive
- Concave: negative

## Combinations of Lenses

P_total = P₁ + P₂ + ...
1/f_total = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂

## Human Eye

- Cornea + lens focus light on retina
- Accommodation: lens changes shape
- Near point: 25 cm
- Far point: infinity

## Defects

- Myopia: focal point in front of retina (concave lens)
- Hypermetropia: focal point behind retina (convex lens)
- Presbyopia: loss of accommodation
learning_objectives: Understand lens types and image formation
